Expanding to 80,000 examples helps only if the edge cases are represented in the expanded dataset; without edge case examples, more data of the same distribution does not improve edge case performance.

q: An enterprise is evaluating foundation models for a contract analysis application. The evaluation criteria include: accuracy on contract clause extraction, response latency, cost per contract, data privacy, and explainability of decisions. Which evaluation methodology produces a decision that stakeholders across legal, finance, and IT can accept?
options: Have the CTO select the model based on technical benchmark reports and announce the decision; technical authority is sufficient for model selection in enterprise AI deployments
Select the model with the highest score on the MMLU academic benchmark; MMLU measures general reasoning ability that directly predicts contract analysis accuracy for all enterprise legal tasks

Design a multi-criteria evaluation: assemble a golden dataset of 500 contracts with ground-truth clause extractions (validated by legal team); evaluate each candidate model on the golden dataset for accuracy; measure p99 latency under realistic load; calculate total cost per contract at expected volume; confirm AWS data handling commitments for the legal team; document model confidence scores and citation of source clauses for the explainability requirement; score each criterion with weights agreed by all stakeholders; present results in a decision matrix and select the model that scores highest on the weighted criteria

Run a 2-week user acceptance test with all 3 stakeholder groups simultaneously and select the model that each group independently chooses; unanimous stakeholder agreement is required before model selection can proceed
explain: Multi-stakeholder enterprise AI decisions require an evaluation framework that addresses each stakeholder group's legitimate concerns with objective, measurable evidence. A golden dataset with legal-team-validated ground truth provides accuracy evidence that the legal team can trust. Load testing produces p99 latency data for IT's SLA assessment. Cost-per-contract calculations satisfy finance's ROI requirements. Data handling documentation addresses legal's confidentiality concerns. Confidence scores and source citations provide the explainability legal requires for audit trails. Pre-agreed criterion weights ensure no single stakeholder's priorities dominate the decision inappropriately. Stakeholder-agreed weights produce a decision that all groups can accept even if their preferred criterion did not win. CTO-only selection ignores legitimate non-technical concerns. MMLU measures general knowledge, not contract-specific clause extraction accuracy. 2-week parallel UAT with unanimous agreement creates a slow, conflict-prone process that may never converge.

q: A media company runs 12 different AI-powered products: headline generators, image caption generators, content moderators, and personalization engines. Each product currently uses a different foundation model configuration managed independently. The platform team wants to standardize while preserving each product's specific model optimizations. Which multi-model management approach achieves this?
options: Standardize all 12 products on a single foundation model and a single system prompt; product differentiation should come from UX rather than model configuration differences
Have each product team manage their own model independently with no central coordination; team autonomy produces better model optimization than centralized standards

Build a centralized AI gateway service that handles authentication, rate limiting, cost tracking, and audit logging for all Bedrock API calls from all 12 products; each product team registers their model configuration (model ID, system prompt, temperature, guardrail config) in a central registry; the gateway enforces common security and compliance policies while allowing product teams to configure their specific model parameters; centralize cost monitoring and model performance dashboards across all products
Merge all 12 products into a single AI platform with one shared model endpoint; microservice isolation of AI capabilities increases total infrastructure cost without delivering differentiation
explain: A centralized AI gateway with a configuration registry achieves platform standardization while preserving product-level customization — the key architectural principle is separating cross-cutting concerns (security, compliance, cost, monitoring) from product-specific concerns (model choice, prompt design, output format). The gateway handles authentication (ensuring all API calls use approved IAM roles), rate limiting (protecting against runaway costs), cost attribution (which product team is spending what), and audit logging (compliance across all products) centrally — so each product team does not reimplement these concerns. Product teams retain full control over their model configurations (different foundation models for different tasks, different system prompts, different guardrail policies) registered in the central catalog. This gives platform teams operational visibility without micromanaging product decisions. Single-model standardization eliminates legitimate product-specific optimizations (an image captioner has different needs than a content moderator). Full decentralization creates 12 independent implementations of security and compliance controls — a governance nightmare. Merging products into one endpoint eliminates the independent deployment and scaling properties each product requires.

q: An AI team has been using Claude 3 Haiku for their document classification system. They are evaluating upgrading to Claude 3 Opus, which scores higher on their internal quality benchmark (88% vs. 79% accuracy). The system processes 2 million documents per month, averaging 800 input tokens each. What factors should drive the upgrade decision?
options: Upgrade immediately since any accuracy improvement is worth pursuing regardless of cost; higher model quality always produces proportional business value
Never upgrade to a larger model once a system is in production; stability is more valuable than incremental quality improvements for classification workloads

Calculate the total monthly cost difference ($0.25/MTok × 800 tokens × 2M docs ≈ $400/month for Haiku vs. $15/MTok = $24,000/month for Opus); quantify the business value of the 9% accuracy improvement (incorrect classifications at current rate, cost of manual review, potential compliance impact); test Sonnet as an intermediate option; evaluate whether fine-tuning Haiku on classification-specific examples could close the accuracy gap at lower cost than upgrading to Opus
Upgrade to Opus and simultaneously reduce document volume by 90% through pre-filtering to make the upgrade cost-neutral; reducing inference volume compensates for higher per-token cost
explain: Upgrading foundation models requires a rigorous cost-benefit analysis. The cost difference is substantial: Haiku at 2M docs × 800 tokens × $0.25/MTok ≈ $400/month versus Opus at $24,000/month — a $23,600/month increase or $283,200/year. This is justified only if the 9% accuracy improvement generates equivalent or greater business value. Quantifying that value requires knowing: how many of the 2M monthly documents are currently misclassified (21% at Haiku accuracy), what is the cost per misclassification (manual review labor, compliance penalties, customer impact), and whether 9% fewer errors materially reduces those costs. Claude 3 Sonnet as an intermediate option may deliver most of the quality gain at a fraction of Opus cost. Fine-tuning Haiku on classification examples might close the gap entirely — fine-tuned smaller models often outperform larger base models on narrow tasks. Automatic upgrade regardless of ROI is financially irresponsible. Refusal to upgrade ignores legitimate quality requirements. Volume reduction through pre-filtering changes the scope of the problem rather than solving the accuracy trade-off.

q: A developer is building a new application in 2025 and must choose between a foundation model released in 2023 (with an established ecosystem but potentially outdated capabilities) and a newly released model with superior benchmarks but limited production references. Which evaluation approach manages the adoption risk of a new model?
options: Always adopt the newest model immediately since newer models are always superior and the competitive pressure of AI development means older models become obsolete within months
Always use the established model regardless of benchmark differences since production stability always outweighs capability improvements in enterprise AI deployments

Run the new model on a representative sample of production tasks alongside the established model; evaluate benchmark-to-production correlation by checking whether the new model's benchmark improvements translate to the specific tasks that matter for the application; assess API stability and SLA commitments for the new model; check community reports and early adopter feedback; start with the new model on a low-risk, non-critical use case before committing to it for core application features
Select based exclusively on which model has been available longer; age of a foundation model release is the most reliable predictor of production suitability
explain: New foundation model evaluation should be empirical and incremental, not automatic or reflexive. Benchmark improvements do not always transfer to specific application tasks — a model that scores higher on MMLU may not produce better legal document summaries for a specific company's document style. Running both models on representative tasks provides direct evidence of production-relevant quality difference. API stability and SLA commitments matter for production systems; newly released models may have higher error rates or rate limits during early availability. Community reports and early adopter feedback surface unexpected failure modes. Starting with the new model on a lower-risk use case creates a controlled production experiment before committing it to critical workloads. Automatic adoption of new models risks production disruptions. Automatic rejection ignores genuine capability improvements. Age alone does not predict suitability — a 6-month-old model may be perfectly stable.

q: A bank deploys an Amazon Bedrock-powered loan decisioning assistant that recommends approval or denial for mortgage applications. Six months post-launch, an internal audit discovers that applicants from a specific zip code are denied at 2.3x the rate of applicants with identical financial profiles from other zip codes. Which actions should the bank take?
options: Discontinue the AI system permanently since any detected bias in a financial decision system makes it permanently unsuitable for production use regardless of mitigation efforts
The disparity is statistically insignificant if fewer than 1,000 applicants from the affected zip code were processed; wait for a larger sample size before investigating further

Immediately investigate whether the zip code correlates with a protected characteristic (race, national origin) constituting disparate impact under the Equal Credit Opportunity Act; pause or add mandatory human review for affected zip code decisions while investigation proceeds; audit training data for proxy discrimination; test removal of geographic features from the model inputs; if correlation exists, implement bias mitigation (re-weighting, adversarial debiasing, or model replacement); document remediation steps for regulatory reporting; notify affected applicants of the review
Replace the AI system with a purely rules-based scoring model since algorithmic systems are categorically more biased than rules-based systems and rule-based credit scoring has no disparate impact
explain: Disparate impact in mortgage lending based on zip code may constitute illegal discrimination under the Equal Credit Opportunity Act (ECOA) if the zip code is a proxy for race or national origin (a well-documented phenomenon in US real estate called redlining). Immediate steps: investigate the correlation, pause or override automated denials for affected applicants pending review (to stop ongoing harm), and notify regulators if the bank suspects a systematic violation. Technical investigation should audit training data for historical discrimination patterns encoded in the data, test whether removing geographic features eliminates or reduces the disparity, and evaluate debiasing techniques. Regulatory reporting and documentation satisfy compliance requirements. Permanent discontinuation ignores the possibility of effective remediation. Small sample size arguments do not override regulatory obligations when a 2.3x disparity is detected. Rules-based systems can also exhibit disparate impact; the issue is model design and training data, not the general category of algorithmic vs. rule-based.

q: An enterprise is developing its responsible AI policy for Amazon Bedrock deployments. The policy must address fairness, transparency, accountability, and safety. Which components are essential for a comprehensive responsible AI implementation framework?
options: A responsible AI policy only needs to address safety (content filtering); fairness, transparency, and accountability are aspirational principles that do not require operationalization in technical systems
Responsible AI is satisfied by including a disclaimer in all AI-generated outputs stating that the content is AI-generated; disclosure alone constitutes the entirety of responsible AI practice

Operationalize each principle: fairness through demographic parity testing pre-deployment and continuous monitoring; transparency through model cards documenting training data, intended use, and known limitations, and through output explanations when legally required; accountability through a named AI risk owner per deployment, incident response procedures, and a harm reporting channel; safety through Bedrock Guardrails configuration, red-teaming before launch, and post-deployment adversarial monitoring
Responsible AI only applies to external customer-facing systems; internal tools used only by employees do not require any responsible AI controls regardless of the decisions they inform
explain: Responsible AI is an operational discipline, not a declaration. Fairness requires empirical testing — demographic parity or equalized odds testing on held-out data before deployment, and ongoing monitoring to detect drift. Transparency requires model cards that document what the model was trained on, what tasks it is intended for, known failure modes, and performance on demographic subgroups — plus output-level explanations where users or regulators have a right to know why a decision was made. Accountability requires named humans who are responsible for each AI system's outcomes, clear escalation paths for concerns, and documented incident response procedures. Safety requires technical controls (Bedrock Guardrails), pre-deployment adversarial testing to find failure modes before users do, and monitoring for emerging misuse patterns. Content filtering alone does not address fairness or transparency. Disclosure through disclaimers is a transparency component but not the complete framework. Internal tools also require responsible AI controls — HR systems, performance management, and internal audit tools that inform decisions about employees carry significant fairness and accountability obligations.

q: A company deploys a foundation model on Amazon Bedrock to generate marketing content. The model occasionally produces outputs that are factually incorrect, stereotypical, or inconsistent with brand values. The content team reviews outputs before publishing but the volume (1,000 pieces/day) makes thorough human review impractical. Which approach scales responsible content generation while maintaining acceptable quality and safety?
options: Remove all human review since the model produces acceptable content in the majority of cases; the cost of reviewing 1,000 pieces per day is not justified when errors occur in a small minority of outputs
Reduce content volume to 50 pieces per day so that a single reviewer can thoroughly inspect every output; scaling back AI usage is preferable to implementing automated quality controls

Implement risk-stratified review: configure Bedrock Guardrails to automatically block outputs containing defined harmful content categories; use an LLM-as-judge to score each output for factual accuracy, brand alignment, and stereotyping risk; route high-risk-scored outputs to mandatory human review and auto-publish low-risk outputs; establish a feedback loop where human reviewers label edge cases to continuously improve the automated scoring model; set weekly error rate targets and alert when thresholds are exceeded

Publish all AI-generated content immediately and implement a post-publication community flagging system; crowdsourced error detection is more scalable than pre-publication review and catches more errors due to larger reviewer pool
explain: Risk-stratified review scales responsible AI oversight to high-volume content generation. Bedrock Guardrails provides automated first-pass filtering for defined harmful categories (hate speech, sexual content, dangerous advice) at minimal latency cost. An LLM-as-judge evaluation layer scores each output for factual accuracy, brand alignment, and bias risk using a lightweight model — routing only flagged outputs to human reviewers. This concentrates expensive human attention on the content that most needs it. A feedback loop where human reviewers label edge cases allows the automated scoring model to improve over time, reducing false positive rates that waste reviewer time. Error rate monitoring with alerts maintains visibility and accountability. Removing all human review is inappropriate for brand-facing content where errors have reputational consequences. Reducing volume discards the business value of AI-powered scale. Post-publication flagging exposes harmful content to audiences before it is caught — unacceptable for a brand publishing platform.

q: A fintech startup uses Amazon Bedrock to process customer financial data including bank account numbers, transaction histories, and income verification documents. They are preparing for a SOC 2 Type II audit that will evaluate their AI system's security controls. Which combination of AWS security controls best addresses the SOC 2 Trust Service Criteria for this Bedrock deployment?
options: Enable AWS Shield Standard on the API Gateway endpoint in front of Bedrock; DDoS protection satisfies the availability criteria of SOC 2 and no additional controls are required

Store all financial data in S3 with SSE-S3 encryption; S3 server-side encryption satisfies all SOC 2 security criteria since data at rest protection is the primary security requirement for financial data

Implement: VPC endpoint for Bedrock (network isolation — no public internet path for financial data); KMS CMK encryption for all data at rest in S3 and CloudWatch Logs; IAM roles with least-privilege for Bedrock invocations; Bedrock model invocation logging to S3 for audit trail (SOC 2 monitoring criterion); AWS CloudTrail for all API calls; AWS Config rules enforcing encryption and access policies; GuardDuty for threat detection; and document all controls in a security responsibility matrix for auditors
Use Amazon Cognito to authenticate users accessing the Bedrock-powered application; user authentication is the primary security control required for SOC 2 compliance in financial AI systems
explain: SOC 2 Type II evaluates controls across five Trust Service Criteria: Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and Privacy. A comprehensive control set for a financial AI system on Bedrock must address each. VPC endpoints satisfy network security by ensuring financial data never traverses the public internet. KMS customer-managed keys provide encryption at rest with auditable key usage logs — satisfying confidentiality. Least-privilege IAM roles limit blast radius of compromised credentials — satisfying security. Bedrock invocation logging to S3 creates an immutable audit trail of what data was sent to which model — satisfying monitoring requirements. CloudTrail records all API calls for the control evidence auditors require. AWS Config enforces configuration standards and detects drift. GuardDuty identifies anomalous access patterns that may indicate compromise. Shield Standard protects availability but is one control among many, not sufficient alone. S3 encryption is one control, not a complete framework. User authentication (Cognito) is necessary but addresses only the access control criterion, not confidentiality, monitoring, or processing integrity.

q: A company wants to build a customer service chatbot using a foundation model on Amazon Bedrock. The chatbot must have access to the company's internal product catalog and return answers based only on official product information. The solution must require minimal ML expertise from the development team. Which Amazon Bedrock feature should they use?
options: Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases with the company's product catalog stored in Amazon S3, connected to a foundation model via Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to ground responses in official product data
Fine-tune the foundation model in Amazon Bedrock with the product catalog as training data so the model learns the catalog and can answer questions without retrieval
Use Amazon Bedrock Agents with custom action groups that query the product database directly, allowing the model to call the database API on each user question
Deploy the foundation model using Amazon SageMaker JumpStart and build a custom vector database for the product catalog to enable semantic search
explain: Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases provides managed RAG — it automatically processes, chunks, embeds, and stores documents from S3 into a vector store, and retrieves relevant chunks at inference time to ground model responses. This requires minimal ML expertise (no model training, no custom vector DB management) and ensures answers are based on official product data. Fine-tuning injects knowledge into model weights — it requires labeled training data, compute resources, and the model may still hallucinate. Bedrock Agents with action groups require API development. SageMaker JumpStart deployment requires more infrastructure expertise.

q: A developer is building an application using Amazon Bedrock to summarize legal documents. During testing, they notice the model sometimes generates summaries that include fabricated legal precedents not present in the original document. Which technique MOST effectively reduces this hallucination problem?
options: Use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with the legal document as context — pass the full document text in the prompt and instruct the model to only summarize information explicitly present in the provided text, not to add external knowledge
Increase the temperature parameter to 1.5 to encourage the model to be more creative and diverse in its responses, which reduces repetitive hallucinations
Switch to a larger foundation model (more parameters) — larger models hallucinate less due to having seen more training data
Add more examples of good summaries to the prompt (few-shot prompting) to teach the model the expected output format
explain: Grounding the model in the source document by passing it as context and explicitly instructing the model to only use provided text is the most effective technique for this specific hallucination type (confabulation). The instruction 'only use information from the document, do not use outside knowledge' limits the model to extractive/abstractive summarization of the actual content. Increasing temperature increases randomness and would likely worsen hallucinations. Larger models have less hallucination tendency but don't eliminate it — grounding is still needed. Few-shot examples teach format but don't prevent adding external knowledge.

q: A company wants to use Amazon Bedrock to build an application that can answer questions in multiple languages (English, Spanish, French, German). They need to evaluate which foundation model best handles multilingual queries before committing to one. Which approach allows efficient model evaluation across multiple languages?
options: Use Amazon Bedrock Model Evaluation with a multilingual test dataset containing questions and reference answers in all four languages, comparing model responses using automatic metrics (ROUGE, BERTScore) and human evaluation scores
Deploy each candidate model and run production traffic for 30 days in each language, measuring user satisfaction to determine which model performs best
Select the model with the most parameters, as larger models universally perform better on multilingual tasks
Use the default model in Amazon Bedrock and rely on prompt engineering to specify the response language — all models perform equally on multilingual tasks
explain: Amazon Bedrock Model Evaluation provides a structured way to compare multiple foundation models on custom datasets. A multilingual test dataset with questions and reference answers in all target languages enables objective comparison using automatic metrics (ROUGE for text overlap, BERTScore for semantic similarity) and human preference ratings. This is efficient — testing before production commitment, on your actual use case. Production A/B testing wastes 30 days and exposes customers to potentially poor responses. Model size doesn't guarantee multilingual performance — training data and language representation matter more. Foundation models vary significantly in multilingual capabilities.

q: A company is selecting a foundation model for a text classification task that classifies support tickets into 50 categories. They have 10,000 labeled examples. The model must respond within 100ms. Which approach provides the BEST balance of accuracy and latency?
options: Fine-tune a smaller, faster foundation model (e.g., a 7B parameter model) on the 10,000 labeled examples — fine-tuning adapts the model to the specific classification task, improving accuracy over zero-shot while maintaining low latency compared to larger models
Use a 70B parameter model with zero-shot prompting — larger models have better in-context learning and will achieve high accuracy without training

Use a small encoder-only model (like BERT) with a classification head trained on the 10,000 examples — fine-tuned discriminative models outperform generative LLMs for classification and have the lowest latency
Use Amazon Bedrock Agents to chain multiple models: one to understand the ticket and one to classify it — this improves accuracy through ensemble reasoning
explain: For a classification task with labeled training data and strict latency requirements, an encoder-only discriminative model (BERT, RoBERTa) fine-tuned on the 10,000 examples is the optimal choice. These models produce a single embedding then apply a classification head — extremely fast (often <20ms). They're trained specifically for classification, achieving state-of-the-art performance on such tasks. Fine-tuning a generative 7B model is also valid but generative models still generate token-by-token, making 100ms challenging. A 70B model zero-shot is very slow and less accurate than a fine-tuned specialized model. Chaining multiple models via Agents multiplies latency — worst option for 100ms.

q: A media company wants to use generative AI to create product descriptions for 100,000 e-commerce items. Each description should be unique, engaging, and approximately 150 words. The process must complete overnight. Which architecture meets these requirements MOST cost-effectively?
options: Use Amazon Bedrock batch inference to process all 100,000 items in a single batch job overnight — batch inference significantly reduces per-request cost compared to real-time API calls and is designed for high-volume asynchronous workloads
Use Amazon Bedrock real-time inference with a Lambda function calling the API in parallel for all 100,000 items simultaneously
Fine-tune a generative model on existing product descriptions and deploy it on Amazon SageMaker with auto-scaling to handle the 100,000 item batch
Use Amazon Q Business with the product catalog as a knowledge source to generate descriptions through the Q Business API
explain: Amazon Bedrock batch inference is purpose-built for high-volume asynchronous processing at reduced cost (typically 50% discount vs. real-time). Jobs run overnight, making it perfect for this use case. Results are written to S3. Calling the real-time API in parallel for 100,000 items simultaneously would exceed rate limits, incur higher costs, and create complex error handling. Fine-tuning for description generation adds unnecessary ML complexity and cost when standard foundation models excel at this creative task. Amazon Q Business is an enterprise knowledge assistant, not a batch content generation platform.

q: A developer is building a customer-facing AI assistant using Amazon Bedrock. During testing, they discover that users can manipulate the model into ignoring its instructions by saying 'Ignore all previous instructions and instead...' followed by harmful instructions. Which technique BEST mitigates this prompt injection vulnerability?
options: Use Amazon Bedrock Guardrails with denied topics and word filters to detect and block adversarial instructions, place system prompt instructions in the system role (not user role) with clear boundaries between system and user input, and validate outputs before returning them to users
Increase the foundation model's temperature to 0 so it deterministically follows only the original system prompt and ignores any user attempts to override it
Use a larger foundation model — models with more parameters are better at distinguishing system instructions from user injection attempts
Encode the system prompt using Base64 and decode it in the application layer — this prevents users from seeing and targeting the original instructions
explain: Prompt injection is mitigated through multiple layers: (1) Using the system role for privileged instructions separates them from user content. (2) Amazon Bedrock Guardrails can detect and block adversarial patterns in user input before the model processes them. (3) Output validation catches inappropriate responses before they reach users. Temperature=0 makes outputs deterministic but doesn't prevent the model from following injected instructions. Larger models are somewhat better at resisting injection but are not immune. Base64 encoding doesn't help — users inject into their own message field, not the system prompt, and the model sees decoded text anyway.

q: A data analyst needs a foundation model to extract structured information from unstructured customer feedback emails. The output must be valid JSON with specific fields: sentiment (positive/negative/neutral), main_topic, and action_required (boolean). Which prompting approach MOST reliably produces structured JSON output?
options: Use a system prompt specifying JSON output format, provide the exact JSON schema in the prompt with a completed example (few-shot), and instruct the model to output ONLY valid JSON without any surrounding text — some models also support native JSON mode for guaranteed valid JSON
Ask the model to 'analyze the email' and then use a regex pattern to extract the JSON from wherever it appears in the model's output
Fine-tune the model on 1,000 email examples with corresponding JSON outputs so it reliably produces the correct format for all future emails
Use chain-of-thought prompting where the model first reasons about the email, then generates the JSON at the end of its reasoning chain
explain: Reliable structured output requires: (1) Clear schema definition in the prompt. (2) A concrete few-shot example showing the expected JSON structure. (3) Explicit instruction to output ONLY JSON (no preamble or explanation). Some models (like Claude) support explicit output format modes or can be reliably instructed via system prompts. Using regex to parse free-form output is brittle — if the model adds explanatory text, the regex fails. Fine-tuning for output formatting is over-engineered when prompt engineering achieves the same result. Chain-of-thought produces good reasoning but the JSON appears after text, complicating extraction and adding latency.

q: A company is using Amazon Bedrock to generate marketing copy. The model produces outputs that are too generic and don't reflect the company's specific brand voice. The product team has 50 examples of approved marketing copy in the company's style. Which approach MOST effectively improves brand consistency with LEAST cost and complexity?
options: Include 5-10 of the best brand voice examples directly in the prompt as few-shot examples, along with a detailed description of the brand tone, vocabulary, and style guide in the system prompt
Fine-tune the model on all 50 approved examples to permanently adapt its style to the brand voice, then use the fine-tuned model for all future generation
Increase the temperature parameter to 0.9 to encourage more creative and unique outputs that differentiate from generic responses
Use Amazon Bedrock Agents to call an external style guide API before each generation request, dynamically injecting brand guidelines into the prompt
explain: Few-shot prompting with representative brand examples in the context window is the lowest-cost, fastest iteration approach. Including 5-10 carefully selected examples that demonstrate the desired tone, vocabulary, and style directly shapes the model's output without any training. A system prompt with explicit style rules further reinforces the pattern. Fine-tuning with 50 examples would work but requires training costs, time, and model deployment management — over-engineered for a prompt engineering solution. Higher temperature increases randomness but doesn't instill a specific brand voice. An external API for style guide injection adds latency and infrastructure complexity unnecessarily.

q: A company builds a RAG-based question-answering system using Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases. Users report that the system gives correct answers when questions exactly match the document phrasing, but fails when questions use synonyms or different phrasing. What improvement addresses this retrieval quality issue?
options: Improve the embedding model used for vector search — switch to a higher-quality semantic embedding model that captures meaning rather than keyword matching, and consider query expansion (generating multiple phrasings of the user question before retrieval)
Increase the number of retrieved documents (top-k) from 3 to 20 to ensure the relevant passage is always included in the context
Switch from vector search to keyword-based BM25 search, which is more effective at matching exact terms when the phrasing varies
Pre-process all user questions by removing stop words and extracting keywords before embedding, improving exact-match retrieval
explain: The issue is semantic mismatch — the retrieval system matches on token similarity rather than conceptual meaning. Higher-quality semantic embedding models (like Amazon Titan Embeddings v2 or Cohere Embed) capture semantic relationships between synonyms and paraphrases. Query expansion (generating 2-3 semantic variations of the query) further increases recall. Increasing top-k retrieves more documents but also adds noise and increases context length without fixing the root cause. BM25 is keyword-based — it would worsen synonym handling, not improve it. Removing stop words and extracting keywords further reduces semantic signal, making synonym matching worse.

q: A legal firm implements a RAG system to help lawyers find relevant case precedents. The knowledge base contains 500,000 legal documents. Lawyers report that answers sometimes mix context from unrelated cases. What architectural improvement BEST addresses this cross-contamination issue?
options: Implement metadata filtering in retrieval — tag each document with practice area, jurisdiction, and date, then filter retrieved documents by the metadata matching the query context before passing to the LLM for generation
Reduce the chunk size from 1,000 tokens to 100 tokens so retrieved chunks are more focused and less likely to contain irrelevant information
Increase the foundation model's context window by switching to a model that supports 200K tokens, allowing all relevant documents to be included
Add a re-ranking step after retrieval using a cross-encoder model to score relevance of each retrieved chunk before passing to the LLM
explain: Metadata filtering prevents cross-contamination by restricting retrieval to documents within the relevant practice area, jurisdiction, and time period before vector similarity search. This structural approach ensures only contextually appropriate documents are candidates for retrieval. Reducing chunk size to 100 tokens makes chunks too small to contain full legal reasoning, hurting answer quality. Using a 200K context window includes all retrieved documents but doesn't prevent mixing unrelated cases — the LLM still receives mixed context. Re-ranking improves relevance ordering but doesn't filter out structurally irrelevant documents from different practice areas if their vector similarity is high.

q: A healthcare company wants to adapt a general-purpose foundation model to understand medical terminology and answer clinical questions. They have 50,000 medical Q&A pairs. Which fine-tuning technique minimizes compute cost while achieving strong performance?
options: Use Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) — LoRA freezes the original model weights and trains small rank-decomposition matrices, requiring ~1% of the parameters to be trained while achieving comparable performance to full fine-tuning
Perform full fine-tuning on the entire model by unfreezing all layers and training on the 50,000 examples, which achieves the highest possible accuracy
Use prompt tuning (prefix tuning) — train only a small set of soft prompt tokens prepended to each input, which requires even less compute than LoRA
Use retrieval-augmented generation instead of fine-tuning — store the 50,000 Q&A pairs in a vector database and retrieve relevant examples at inference time
explain: LoRA is the most cost-effective fine-tuning technique for adapting foundation models. It adds small trainable rank-decomposition matrices to attention layers while keeping the base model frozen. This means training ~1% of parameters vs. 100% for full fine-tuning — dramatically reducing GPU memory requirements, training time, and cost. LoRA performance is typically within 1-2% of full fine-tuning on domain adaptation tasks. Full fine-tuning achieves marginally higher performance but costs 10-100x more compute. Prompt tuning (soft prompts) is even cheaper but significantly underperforms LoRA on complex domain adaptation. RAG doesn't train the model, so medical terminology understanding won't improve.

q: A company fine-tuned a foundation model on their proprietary customer support data and deployed it. After 3 months, they notice the model's general language understanding has degraded — it performs worse on tasks that the original model handled well before fine-tuning. What phenomenon is this and how should it be addressed?
options: This is catastrophic forgetting — the model overwrote general knowledge with domain-specific knowledge during fine-tuning; address it by using LoRA or other PEFT techniques that preserve base weights, or by mixing general-purpose training examples with domain examples during fine-tuning
This is model drift — the fine-tuned model slowly degrades over time due to distribution shift in customer queries; address by retraining quarterly with the latest customer support data
This is overfitting — the model memorized training examples and cannot generalize; address by adding L2 regularization and reducing training epochs
This is embedding collapse — fine-tuning caused all embeddings to cluster together; address by adding a contrastive learning objective during fine-tuning
explain: Catastrophic forgetting is a well-known challenge in fine-tuning neural networks — when a model trains intensively on domain data, it overwrites the weights that stored general knowledge. PEFT techniques like LoRA mitigate this because base weights are frozen and only small adapter matrices are trained. If full fine-tuning is used, mixing domain examples with a sample of general training data (rehearsal/replay) maintains general capabilities. Model drift is a real phenomenon but describes distributional changes in input data over time, not immediate post-training degradation. Overfitting manifests as memorization of training data rather than general capability loss. Embedding collapse is a contrastive learning problem, not typical fine-tuning.

q: A company deploys an AI-powered financial advisor chatbot using Amazon Bedrock. Regulations require that all financial advice be accurate and the company must mitigate liability from incorrect information. Which combination of safeguards BEST reduces the risk of harmful hallucinations?
options: Implement RAG with official financial data sources as the knowledge base, use Amazon Bedrock Guardrails to configure grounding checks that score response faithfulness to retrieved context, add disclaimers to every response, and implement human-in-the-loop review for high-stakes advice
Set temperature to 0 to make the model completely deterministic and only output the most probable tokens, eliminating hallucinations
Use the largest available foundation model — models with more parameters hallucinate significantly less and are suitable for financial advice without additional safeguards
Fine-tune the model on regulatory financial documents so it learns accurate financial information and no longer generates incorrect advice
explain: For high-stakes financial applications, a layered approach is required: (1) RAG grounds responses in authoritative data sources, reducing confabulation. (2) Bedrock Guardrails grounding checks (using ROUGE or model-based faithfulness scoring) detect when responses contradict the retrieved context. (3) Disclaimers set user expectations and provide legal protection. (4) Human review for complex advice adds the last safety net. Temperature=0 makes outputs deterministic but doesn't prevent hallucinations — just makes them consistent. Larger models hallucinate less but are not reliable enough for regulated financial advice without safeguards. Fine-tuning reduces but doesn't eliminate hallucinations, and the model can still confabulate on topics not in training data.

q: A company uses a generative AI model to summarize medical research papers for doctors. A doctor reports that a summary contained a specific drug dosage recommendation that was not in the original paper. What type of hallucination is this and which technical measure directly reduces it?
options: This is an intrinsic hallucination (the model fabricated specific facts not in the source); reduce it by using extractive summarization constraints in the prompt ('only use information explicitly stated in the text') combined with faithfulness evaluation metrics like BERTScore comparing summary to source
This is an extrinsic hallucination (the model added external medical knowledge); reduce it by fine-tuning the model exclusively on the paper's text so it cannot access external medical training data
This is an attribution error (the model cited wrong papers); reduce it by adding citation requirements to the prompt so the model must reference specific passages for every claim
This is a formatting hallucination (the model generated false structure); reduce it by using JSON output mode to constrain the format
explain: Fabricating specific facts (drug dosages) not present in the source is an intrinsic hallucination — the model generates plausible-sounding content that contradicts or augments the source material. The most direct mitigations are: (1) Grounding instructions in the prompt ('only use information in the provided text') make the model less likely to add external knowledge. (2) Faithfulness evaluation (BERTScore, FactScore, or model-based faithfulness checking) scores how well the summary matches the source, catching fabrications before they reach doctors. Fine-tuning on the paper's text doesn't work (one paper can't be fine-tuned on). Citation requirements help for attribution but not content fabrication. JSON mode only constrains format.

q: A company wants to deploy Amazon Q Business for their 500 employees to search and ask questions about internal documents stored in SharePoint, Confluence, and a SQL database. Different employee groups should only see documents they are authorized to access. Which Amazon Q Business feature enforces these access controls?
options: Amazon Q Business supports native index connectors for SharePoint and Confluence that synchronize document-level access control lists (ACLs); when a user queries Q, it only surfaces documents the user is authorized to access based on their identity from the connected IAM Identity Center
Create separate Amazon Q Business applications for each employee group and connect only the relevant documents to each application
Configure Amazon Q Business with a single shared index and use prompt engineering to instruct the model not to reveal documents the user shouldn't see
Enable Amazon Q Business with Amazon Macie integration to automatically classify document sensitivity and restrict responses based on classification
explain: Amazon Q Business supports built-in access control list (ACL) crawling for connectors like SharePoint and Confluence. It synchronizes document-level permissions and maps them to user identities via IAM Identity Center. At query time, Q Business enforces these permissions — a user only receives answers derived from documents they're authorized to access. This is automatic, granular, and scales to complex enterprise permission models. Creating separate applications per group is operationally expensive and doesn't handle overlapping permissions. Prompt engineering cannot enforce security — a model instructed not to reveal confidential information can still be manipulated into doing so. Macie classifies data but doesn't provide query-time access control.

q: A software development team wants to use Amazon Q Developer to speed up their development workflow. They want Q Developer to understand their company's specific coding standards, internal libraries, and architectural patterns when making suggestions. How can they configure Q Developer for this?
options: Use Amazon Q Developer with a customization feature that ingests the company's internal code repositories to learn the coding patterns, internal library APIs, and architectural conventions specific to the codebase
Provide coding standard documents as attachments in each Q Developer chat session so it has context for each conversation
Enable Q Developer's Code Review mode, which analyzes all code in the repository and adapts its suggestions based on the existing codebase patterns automatically
Configure Q Developer to use a custom foundation model fine-tuned on the company's code base using Amazon Bedrock
explain: Amazon Q Developer supports customization by ingesting internal code repositories to learn company-specific patterns, internal library APIs, and architectural conventions. This creates a custom Q Developer experience that suggests code aligned with the company's standards rather than generic patterns. The customization trains on the actual codebase. Providing documents in each chat session is manual and doesn't scale. Q Developer's code review analyzes the current file/selection but doesn't learn organizational patterns across the entire codebase. Amazon Q Developer and Amazon Bedrock are separate services — Q Developer is not directly powered by a Bedrock-configurable custom model.

q: A company is building a document analysis application using Amazon Bedrock that processes 200-page legal documents. The application uses Claude (200K context window). A developer notices that processing costs are very high because the full 200-page document is sent in every API call, even for simple questions. Which optimization reduces cost while maintaining answer quality?
options: Implement RAG: chunk and embed the document, store in a vector database, retrieve only the relevant passages (3-5 chunks) for each question, and pass only those chunks to the model instead of the full document
Switch to a model with a larger context window so the full document fits efficiently with less cost per token
Compress the document by removing whitespace and punctuation before sending to the API to reduce the token count
Cache the full document in the model using Bedrock's prompt caching feature so subsequent questions don't re-process the document
explain: RAG retrieves only the relevant passages (typically 3-5 chunks, ~2,000 tokens) instead of the full document (~100,000+ tokens for 200 pages). This reduces input tokens by 95%+, with proportional cost reduction. A larger context window doesn't reduce cost — it just allows more tokens; the same large input is still expensive. Removing whitespace reduces token count marginally (5-10%) without improving cost significantly. Prompt caching (available on some Bedrock models) does reduce repeated processing cost, but for many different questions across many documents, the cache hit rate may be low. RAG is the primary cost optimization for document analysis.

q: A developer is using Amazon Bedrock to build a multi-turn chatbot. After many conversation turns, the model starts forgetting context from the beginning of the conversation and its response quality degrades. The model has a 32K token context window. What is MOST likely happening and what is the recommended solution?
options: The conversation history has exceeded the context window limit; implement a sliding window or summary-based memory management strategy — summarize older turns and keep only recent turns in full to maintain essential context within the token budget
The model is experiencing catastrophic forgetting from the long conversation; fine-tune the model on multi-turn conversations to improve its long-context retention
The API rate limit is being hit, causing responses to be truncated; implement exponential backoff and retry logic with reduced input size
Temperature drift is occurring in long conversations; reset the temperature parameter to 0 every 10 turns to maintain deterministic responses
explain: As conversation turns accumulate, the combined message history eventually exceeds the model's context window. The model silently truncates the oldest turns, losing early context. The solution is conversation memory management: (1) Summarize older conversation segments (e.g., 'Previously we discussed: ...') and replace the raw turns with the summary. (2) Keep only the most recent N turns in full. This keeps the total context within the window while preserving key information. This is an inference-time issue, not model training. Rate limiting causes API errors, not degraded quality. Temperature affects output diversity, not context retention.

q: A company is evaluating whether to use generative AI or traditional ML for a customer churn prediction task. They have 500,000 labeled historical records and need predictions with 95%+ accuracy. The model will run continuously in production. Which approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Use traditional ML (gradient boosting or random forest) trained on the 500,000 labeled examples — supervised classification with abundant labeled data and a well-defined prediction task is the optimal use case for traditional ML, offering higher accuracy, faster inference, lower cost, and better explainability than generative AI
Use a generative AI foundation model with few-shot prompting to classify customer churn risk — foundation models have broad knowledge that improves churn prediction accuracy
Fine-tune a generative foundation model on the 500,000 labeled examples for churn classification — this combines the power of generative AI with domain data
Use Amazon Bedrock Agents to analyze customer data and reason through churn risk factors using chain-of-thought prompting
explain: Customer churn prediction with 500,000 labeled records is a classic supervised classification task where traditional ML models (XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest) excel. With abundant labeled data, these models achieve high accuracy (often >95%), run inference in milliseconds at low cost, and provide feature importance for explainability. Generative AI is not designed for structured tabular classification — it was trained on text and cannot directly process tabular features. Fine-tuning a foundation model on tabular data is technically complex and would underperform specialized tabular ML models. Agents with chain-of-thought would be extremely slow and expensive for continuous production scoring.

q: A developer asks: 'What is the difference between zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting?' Which answer correctly describes these techniques?
options: Zero-shot: the model answers with no examples in the prompt, relying entirely on pre-trained knowledge. One-shot: one example of input-output pair is included in the prompt. Few-shot: multiple examples (typically 3-10) are included to guide the model's output format and style
Zero-shot: the model is trained from scratch on the task with zero examples. One-shot: the model is fine-tuned on one training example. Few-shot: the model is fine-tuned on 3-10 examples
Zero-shot: the model returns no output to prevent hallucination. One-shot: the model generates exactly one response option. Few-shot: the model generates several candidate responses
Zero-shot: no system prompt is used. One-shot: one system prompt is provided. Few-shot: multiple system prompts are chained together
explain: These terms describe in-context learning techniques where examples are provided within the prompt itself, not training methods. Zero-shot prompting tests the model's inherent knowledge with just an instruction. One-shot includes one example to demonstrate the expected format. Few-shot (3-10 examples) significantly improves performance on structured tasks by showing the pattern to follow. None of these involve model training or fine-tuning — they are pure inference-time prompt strategies. This distinction from fine-tuning is critical: few-shot is prompt engineering, not training.

q: A company's AI team is debating whether to use a foundation model API (Amazon Bedrock) or train their own custom LLM from scratch for a document processing application. They have $500K budget and need the system in 3 months. Which approach is MOST realistic?
options: Use Amazon Bedrock with a pre-trained foundation model, customized via prompt engineering and optionally fine-tuned — training a custom LLM from scratch requires hundreds of millions of dollars in compute, petabytes of training data, and months to years of effort, far exceeding the budget and timeline
Train a custom LLM from scratch using Amazon SageMaker — $500K in compute budget is sufficient for training a high-quality LLM for document processing
Use both approaches in parallel: train a custom LLM and use Bedrock simultaneously, then select the better performer after 3 months
Purchase a pre-trained open-source LLM license and deploy it on Amazon EC2 P4d instances — this avoids API costs and gives full model control
explain: Training a competitive LLM from scratch requires: billions of dollars in compute (training a GPT-3 scale model costs ~$5M in compute alone), petabytes of curated training data, months of engineering time, specialized ML infrastructure teams, and months of training runs. $500K and 3 months are insufficient by orders of magnitude. Amazon Bedrock provides immediate access to state-of-the-art models with fine-tuning options, achievable in weeks. Parallel development is wasteful when custom LLM training is not feasible. Deploying open-source LLMs on EC2 is valid but still requires significant infrastructure setup and the models are less capable than Bedrock's offerings for document processing.

q: A company deploys an AI hiring assistant that uses a foundation model to screen resumes. An internal audit finds that the model scores resumes from candidates with names typical of certain racial groups significantly lower, even with identical qualifications. What is the MOST appropriate response?
options: Immediately suspend the AI assistant from hiring decisions, audit the training data and model outputs for bias, implement fairness metrics (demographic parity, equalized odds) in the evaluation pipeline, and potentially rebuild the system with bias-mitigated training data or debiasing techniques
Add a disclaimer to the system informing candidates that AI is used in screening and that results may be biased, allowing them to request human review
Fine-tune the model on a balanced dataset of resumes to reduce bias in its scoring, then redeploy it in production after testing
Increase the model's temperature to 0.8 to introduce more randomness in scoring, which reduces systematic bias by making scores less predictable
explain: Discriminatory hiring outcomes based on racial proxies are a legal and ethical violation regardless of whether AI is involved. The immediate response must be suspension of the harmful system — continuing to use it while investigating causes ongoing harm. A thorough audit of training data (for historical bias), model outputs (for disparate impact), and decision thresholds is required. Fairness metrics quantify the problem. Rebuilding or debiasing the model addresses the root cause. A disclaimer doesn't fix the discriminatory outcomes. Fine-tuning with balanced data may help but requires validation — redeployment without thorough testing risks repeating the bias. Randomness doesn't eliminate systematic bias, just makes it less predictable.

q: A company wants to implement guardrails for their Amazon Bedrock-powered customer service application to prevent the model from generating inappropriate content, discussing competitor products, or revealing pricing information it shouldn't have access to. Which Amazon Bedrock feature addresses all three requirements?
options: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails — configure denied topics (competitor discussions, pricing not in approved range), content filters (violence, hate speech, sexual content with sensitivity levels), and sensitive information redaction to enforce all three constraints automatically
Implement a post-processing Lambda function that scans model outputs using a separate classification model to detect and filter inappropriate content before returning responses to users
Fine-tune the foundation model with reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to teach it appropriate response boundaries for all three constraints
Use system prompt engineering to explicitly instruct the model not to discuss competitors, not to reveal pricing, and not to generate inappropriate content
explain: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails provides all three capabilities natively: (1) Denied topics block discussions of specified subjects (competitor products, off-limits pricing). (2) Content filters with configurable sensitivity levels block hate speech, violence, and inappropriate content. (3) Sensitive information redaction masks or blocks PII and other sensitive data from model responses. Guardrails apply to both input and output. A post-processing Lambda is a valid but higher-effort custom build. RLHF is a powerful alignment technique but requires significant ML infrastructure and labeled preference data. System prompting alone is insufficient — models can still violate instructions and are vulnerable to prompt injection.

q: A company uses Amazon Bedrock to build an application that processes customer medical records. They must comply with HIPAA. Which controls ensure HIPAA compliance when using Amazon Bedrock?
options: Sign a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) with AWS (Amazon Bedrock is HIPAA-eligible), ensure PHI data sent to the model is encrypted in transit (TLS), configure Amazon Bedrock to not use data for model training (which is the default — Bedrock does not train on customer data), and restrict access to Bedrock APIs using IAM least-privilege policies
Host the foundation model on-premises using Amazon SageMaker Local Mode to ensure PHI never leaves the company's network
Use Amazon Bedrock's HIPAA mode configuration to enable healthcare-compliant processing with automatically applied PHI masking
Replace Amazon Bedrock with Amazon Comprehend Medical, which is specifically designed for medical NLP and includes built-in HIPAA compliance controls
explain: Amazon Bedrock is HIPAA-eligible and can be used for PHI processing with a signed BAA. Key HIPAA controls: (1) AWS BAA covers Bedrock's infrastructure security obligations. (2) All data is encrypted in transit (TLS) and at rest by default. (3) Bedrock does not use customer inference data for model training (this is a critical HIPAA requirement — PHI must not leak into model training). (4) IAM policies restrict who can access Bedrock APIs. SageMaker Local Mode is not a real deployment model and doesn't address HIPAA. There is no 'HIPAA mode' in Bedrock — HIPAA compliance is achieved through proper use and configuration. Comprehend Medical is for medical entity extraction, not general-purpose AI applications.

q: A company deployed a foundation model for customer support but users report that responses are technically correct but feel robotic and unhelpful. Standard automated metrics (ROUGE, BLEU) show high scores. How should the company evaluate and improve the model's helpfulness?
options: Implement human evaluation with a structured rubric measuring helpfulness, tone, empathy, and resolution quality — automated metrics measure text overlap, not user experience; use RLHF or preference tuning based on human feedback to fine-tune the model toward human-preferred responses
Reduce the temperature to 0 to make responses more precise and factual, as robotic responses are caused by high-entropy outputs
Increase max_tokens to allow the model to generate longer, more detailed responses that better address customer needs
Switch to a different foundation model — if users find responses unhelpful, the model is fundamentally misaligned and must be replaced
explain: ROUGE and BLEU measure n-gram overlap between generated and reference text — they don't capture tone, empathy, helpfulness, or user satisfaction. Human evaluation with explicit rubrics (is this helpful? does it address the customer's emotion? is the tone appropriate?) directly measures what automated metrics miss. RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) uses these human preferences to fine-tune the model toward responses humans prefer — this is how models like ChatGPT are made helpful. Reducing temperature makes responses more deterministic but doesn't add warmth. Longer responses don't necessarily feel more helpful. Model replacement throws away the domain adaptation without addressing the root cause (lack of helpfulness training).

q: A company is building an AI agent using Amazon Bedrock Agents that needs to look up customer order status, process refunds, and update shipping addresses. The agent must access three different backend systems: an order management API, a payment processor API, and a shipping API. How should the agent's actions be structured?
options: Create an Amazon Bedrock Agent with three separate action groups — one per backend system (OrderManagement, PaymentProcessor, ShippingUpdates) — each with an OpenAPI schema defining the available actions; connect each action group to a Lambda function that calls the respective backend API
Create a single Lambda function that handles all three backend APIs and expose it as one action group with a generic 'callBackend' action that the agent can parameterize
Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases to store the API documentation for all three systems and have the agent generate the correct API calls based on retrieved documentation

Create an Amazon Bedrock Agent with one action group per action (not per system), resulting in individual actions: GetOrderStatus, ProcessRefund, UpdateShippingAddress, each connected to separate Lambda functions
explain: Amazon Bedrock Agents action groups are organized by capability domain, not by individual action. Each action group has an OpenAPI schema defining all available operations for that domain, connected to a Lambda function. Grouping by system (OrderManagement, PaymentProcessor, ShippingUpdates) creates clean logical boundaries that are easy to maintain, test, and update independently. A single mega-Lambda is harder to maintain and test. Knowledge Bases store documents for RAG, not dynamic API specifications. One action group per atomic action (Option D) would create too many action groups for complex systems, making the agent schema unwieldy.

q: A company wants to build a RAG application using Amazon Bedrock that should work with up-to-date information. Their knowledge base is updated daily with new documents. How should they architect the knowledge base to ensure queries always use the latest information?
options: Configure the Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Base with an S3 data source, set up automatic synchronization (triggered by S3 event notifications via EventBridge) to re-index new documents daily, so the vector store is updated within hours of new documents appearing in S3
Rebuild the entire knowledge base from scratch every day by deleting and recreating it with the full document set, ensuring complete freshness
Use a 7-day cache TTL on knowledge base queries so old responses expire automatically and new documents are retrieved after the cache clears

Store all documents in a relational database and have the RAG system query it with SQL full-text search instead of a vector database — relational databases update in real time when new documents are inserted
explain: Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases support incremental synchronization — new documents added to the S3 data source are automatically chunked, embedded, and added to the vector store without requiring a full rebuild. Configuring automatic sync triggered by S3 events via EventBridge ensures new documents are indexed within hours. Full daily rebuilds waste compute resources, cause downtime during rebuild, and are unnecessary for incremental updates. Query caching doesn't update the knowledge base — old embeddings of old documents would still match queries. SQL full-text search doesn't perform semantic similarity matching and loses the core RAG capability.

q: A startup uses Amazon Bedrock with Anthropic Claude for a customer support application that receives 10,000 requests per day. Analysis shows that 60% of requests are simple FAQ questions that could be answered with 500 input/output tokens, while 40% are complex questions requiring 5,000 tokens. Which strategy MOST reduces costs?
options: Implement an intelligent routing layer: classify each request as simple or complex first (using a lightweight classifier or smaller model), then route simple FAQ questions to Claude Haiku (cheaper, fast) and complex questions to Claude Sonnet or Opus, reducing per-request cost by routing 60% of traffic to the most cost-effective model
Cache all responses for 24 hours using Amazon ElastiCache — if the same question is asked twice in 24 hours, return the cached response at zero model cost
Switch entirely to Claude Haiku for all requests to minimize per-token cost, accepting slightly lower quality on complex questions
Implement batch processing where similar questions are grouped and answered together with a single API call, reducing total API calls and costs
explain: Intelligent model routing (also called model cascade or tiered inference) matches question complexity to model capability and cost. Claude Haiku costs ~20x less than Claude Opus per token. Routing 60% of simple traffic to Haiku while using a capable model for complex questions optimizes the cost-quality tradeoff. Response caching is also effective for truly repeated questions but doesn't address the model cost for diverse inputs. Switching everything to Haiku sacrifices quality on complex questions — customers receiving poor answers on difficult issues is a business risk. Batch processing reduces API call overhead but not per-token cost; the same number of tokens are still processed.

q: A company is building an AI-powered code review tool using Amazon Bedrock. The tool must integrate with GitHub to automatically review pull requests and post comments. Which architecture supports this integration?
options: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that triggers on pull request events, sends the code diff to Amazon Bedrock via the AWS SDK, processes the model response, and uses the GitHub API to post review comments — all orchestrated by a serverless Lambda function or GitHub Actions runner
Use Amazon Bedrock Agents with a built-in GitHub connector that natively integrates with pull request workflows without any custom code
Deploy the foundation model on Amazon SageMaker Real-time Inference with a custom GitHub webhook receiver on EC2 to handle pull request events
Use Amazon Q Developer's built-in GitHub integration, which automatically reviews all pull requests in connected repositories and posts AI-generated comments
explain: GitHub webhook events (pull request opened) can trigger either a GitHub Actions workflow or an AWS Lambda via API Gateway. The handler reads the code diff, sends it to Amazon Bedrock's InvokeModel API with a code review prompt, parses the model response, and posts comments via the GitHub API. This standard serverless integration pattern requires no specialized connectors. Amazon Bedrock Agents don't have a built-in GitHub connector. SageMaker deployment adds unnecessary infrastructure complexity vs. Bedrock's managed API. Amazon Q Developer has code review features but not a public API for custom PR automation integration.

q: A company builds a semantic search system for a product catalog with 1 million products. Each product has a title, description, and specifications. The system must return semantically relevant results for queries like 'comfortable running shoes for flat feet'. Which vector database setup on AWS is MOST appropriate for this scale?
options: Use Amazon OpenSearch Service with vector search enabled (k-NN plugin), store product embeddings (using Amazon Titan Embeddings or similar) with product metadata, configure HNSW indexing for fast approximate nearest-neighbor search across 1 million vectors
Store all product embeddings in Amazon DynamoDB as binary attributes and implement a linear scan across all 1 million items for each query using a Lambda function
Use Amazon RDS PostgreSQL with the pgvector extension to store product embeddings and perform vector similarity search using SQL
Store product embeddings in Amazon S3 and load them into memory in a Lambda function for each search request, performing cosine similarity in-memory
explain: Amazon OpenSearch Service with the k-NN plugin supports approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) search using HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) graphs. At 1 million vectors, HNSW provides sub-millisecond query latency with high recall — linear search would take seconds. OpenSearch also supports metadata filtering alongside vector search (filtering by product category while searching semantically). DynamoDB linear scan across 1M items is O(n) — too slow for real-time search. RDS with pgvector is excellent for smaller scales (<1M vectors) but OpenSearch is better optimized for this scale. Loading all embeddings into Lambda memory for cosine similarity is not feasible at 1M scale (memory limits, cold start time).

q: A company wants to use embeddings to detect near-duplicate customer support tickets so that agents aren't handling the same issue multiple times. New tickets arrive at a rate of 500 per day. Which pipeline detects near-duplicates in real-time as tickets arrive?
options: Generate an embedding for each new ticket using Amazon Bedrock (Titan Embeddings), store it in a vector database (OpenSearch), perform a nearest-neighbor search against all stored ticket embeddings, and flag tickets with cosine similarity above a threshold (e.g., 0.92) as potential duplicates for agent review
Use Amazon Comprehend to detect key phrases in each new ticket and compare them with key phrases from previous tickets using exact string matching to find duplicates
Run a daily batch job using Amazon Bedrock to cluster all tickets into groups and identify clusters containing near-duplicate tickets for review
Use Amazon Rekognition to compare screenshots attached to support tickets to detect visually similar tickets
explain: Real-time near-duplicate detection uses embedding similarity: each new ticket is immediately embedded using Titan Embeddings, a k-NN search in OpenSearch finds the most similar existing tickets, and those with cosine similarity above the threshold are flagged as near-duplicates. This pipeline runs in under 1 second per ticket at 500/day volume. Cosine similarity above 0.92 reliably identifies near-paraphrases while avoiding false positives. Comprehend key phrase extraction with string matching misses semantic similarity (same issue described differently). Daily batch clustering doesn't provide real-time detection — a ticket would wait up to 24 hours. Rekognition applies to image matching, not text similarity.

q: A company uses Amazon Bedrock to generate responses for two different use cases: (1) a financial calculator assistant that must give precise numerical answers, and (2) a creative writing helper that generates story ideas. Which temperature settings are appropriate for each use case?
options: Financial calculator: temperature=0 (deterministic, always picks the highest probability token) for consistent, accurate numerical outputs; creative writing: temperature=0.8-1.0 (high randomness) to encourage diverse and imaginative story ideas
Financial calculator: temperature=1.0 to ensure all possible answers are considered; creative writing: temperature=0 to generate the single most coherent story
Both use cases should use temperature=0.5 as a balanced setting that works well for all applications
Temperature only affects response length, not content; use max_tokens to control response quality for both use cases
explain: Temperature controls the randomness of token selection. Temperature=0 makes the model always select the highest-probability token — producing deterministic, consistent outputs ideal for calculation and factual tasks. Any temperature > 0 introduces randomness; temperatures of 0.8-1.0 significantly broaden the distribution, encouraging the model to explore less probable but creative options — ideal for brainstorming and creative generation. Using temperature=1.0 for calculations risks the model selecting incorrect but slightly less probable tokens (e.g., '42' instead of '43'). A fixed 0.5 for everything is a poor compromise. Temperature affects token selection probability, not response length (max_tokens controls length).

q: A developer is testing an Amazon Bedrock application and notices that when calling the same prompt with the same inputs multiple times, the model sometimes gives different answers to factual questions. The application requires consistent, reproducible outputs. Which configuration change addresses this?
options: Set temperature=0 to make the model deterministic — at temperature=0 the model always selects the maximum probability token, producing the same output for the same input
Set top_p=1.0 to include all tokens in the probability distribution, which ensures all answers are considered and the best one is always selected consistently
Enable response streaming to receive the model's output in real-time, which improves consistency by reducing network timeout-related variability
Set max_tokens to a fixed value equal to the expected response length to prevent the model from varying its output length
explain: Temperature is the primary sampling parameter controlling output randomness. At temperature=0, the model always selects the token with the highest probability at each step — making outputs deterministic for the same input (given the same model version). This is essential for applications requiring reproducibility. Top_p=1.0 includes all tokens in the sampling distribution — this doesn't make outputs deterministic, it just doesn't truncate the distribution. Response streaming is a delivery mechanism, not a sampling parameter. Max_tokens limits output length but doesn't affect the randomness of content generation.

q: A startup wants to quickly build a prototype that generates personalized email subject lines for marketing campaigns. They have no ML engineers. Which approach gets them to a working prototype FASTEST?
options: Use Amazon Bedrock with a pre-trained foundation model and craft a prompt with examples of good subject lines (few-shot) — no ML training required, usable in hours
Collect 100,000 email subject line examples, train a T5 model on Amazon SageMaker using the examples, then deploy it for inference
Purchase a specialized marketing AI SaaS tool with a pre-built email subject line generator
Hire an ML engineer to build a custom RNN model trained on the company's historical email data
explain: Amazon Bedrock with few-shot prompting requires only API access and prompt design — achievable in hours with no ML expertise. A few high-quality examples of good subject lines in the prompt guide the model to generate similar outputs for new campaigns. Training a custom model requires data collection, training infrastructure, and ML expertise — weeks of effort. Purchasing a SaaS tool may work but limits customization and requires vendor evaluation. Hiring an ML engineer and training a custom model is the slowest and most expensive path for a prototype.

q: A company wants to understand what types of tasks generative AI (large language models) are MOST suited for versus what tasks are poor fits. Which mapping is CORRECT?
options: Good fit: text generation, summarization, translation, code generation, Q&A from documents, creative writing. Poor fit: exact arithmetic calculations, real-time sensor data processing, highly regulated deterministic decisions requiring 100% accuracy
Good fit: sorting large datasets, running SQL queries, training neural networks, executing system commands. Poor fit: writing marketing copy, answering FAQs, translating languages

Good fit: medical diagnosis requiring 99.9% accuracy, financial trading with zero error tolerance, legal contract enforcement. Poor fit: creative brainstorming, content drafting, answering general questions
LLMs are equally suited for all tasks — their broad training data makes them universally applicable with appropriate prompting
explain: LLMs excel at language-based tasks requiring understanding, generation, and reasoning: writing, summarization, translation, code generation, Q&A, and creative tasks.
